angle.headline: The Hidden Cost of Strategy Without Operating Model
angle.thesis: Mid-market firms are adopting AI point solutions for efficiency but failing to capture strategic value because they haven't redesigned their operating model to support them—the bottleneck is process, not technology.
angle.targetPersona: US-LATAM corridor operators who have deployed AI tools but are disappointed with the ROI and struggling to scale adoption beyond pilot projects
angle.relevanceToAudience: As trade tensions around USMCA rules of origin intensify (sources 3, 5) and competitive pressure from Chinese manufacturing increases (sources 6, 13), US-LATAM operators need technology leverage to maintain their cost advantage. But most are discovering that AI tools alone don't deliver transformation—the operating model must change first. This angle addresses why their AI investments aren't delivering and what sequence of changes will unlock value.

justification.angleChoice: This is the most compelling angle because it addresses a universal pain point for the ICP: investing in technology that fails to deliver promised results. Sources 19 (Kyndryl CHRO) and 10 (AI drug development) provide concrete evidence that AI readiness is declining even as adoption accelerates—a paradox that signals Operating Model breakdown. Source 19 specifically reports that only 23% of leaders say their workforce is fully ready for AI (down 6 points from last year), and 80% expect AI advances will outpace their workforce and operating models. This is not a technology problem—it's a process and capability problem. The Field Report anchor (source 11, ADP CEO) provides a named executive wrestling with this exact question: 'What does a workforce that includes both AI and humans look like?' This angle is superior to other potential angles (CEO burnout, USMCA trade tensions, customer connection in small business) because it directly connects to capital allocation decisions the ICP is making right now and explains why those investments are underperforming.
justification.osPillarChoice: This is Operating Model OS because the dysfunction is not in strategic vision (most firms know they need AI) or in the technology itself (the tools work), but in how work gets executed. Source 19 explicitly identifies the gap: firms have AI but lack redesigned roles, addressed skills gaps, and governance to integrate it. Source 18 on delegation reinforces this—leaders are treating AI adoption like delegation without building capability, leading to disappointment. The bottleneck is process design, decision rights, and capability building—all Operating Model elements. It is not Strategy OS because firms have clarity on the 'why' of AI adoption. It is not Technology OS because the tools are available and functional—the issue is the human system around them has not been reconfigured to capture value.
justification.peopleAngleChoice: The challenge names the specific behavior gap: teams are passively resisting because no one has told them concretely how their role changes—they're waiting for clarity that never comes. Kotter Step 4 (Communicate the Vision) is the precise intervention because leaders have communicated 'we're adopting AI' but not 'here's how your job, decisions, and daily rhythm change as a result.' The concrete action is role redesign documentation and decision rights mapping—not just tool training. Source 19 supports this: Pacesetters (9% of firms) are 'very intentional about building trust, leaning into change management and redesigning processes around talent capabilities.' The framework activates by forcing leaders to translate strategy into tangible workflow changes before deployment, not after.

peopleAngle.challenge: Leaders deployed AI assuming existing workflows would absorb it, but frontline teams lack clarity on how their roles change, creating passive resistance disguised as technical adoption problems.
peopleAngle.framework: Kotter Step 4: Communicate the Vision — Leaders must translate the AI strategy into explicit role redesigns and decision rights for each affected team, showing concretely how daily work changes, not just what new tools are available.

insightClaims.claim: Only 23% of senior business and tech leaders say their workforce is fully ready for AI, a six-percentage-point decrease over the last year, according to a Kyndryl study of 1,100 leaders across eight countries.
Almost 80% of leaders surveyed anticipate the speed of AI advances will outpace their workforce, governance and operating models.
Only one-third of surveyed leaders say AI is fully integrated across their organization.
AI is compressing drug developers' preclinical costs and timelines by as much as 70%, according to a TD Cowen survey of 80 biopharma leaders.
ADP CEO Maria Black identifies what she calls the number one question every chief executive is trying to answer: What does a workforce that includes both AI and humans look like?

Gartner's recent HR trends research notes that AI-driven HR transformation is among HR leaders' top focus areas, even though many say their current HR technologies don't fully meet business needs and about half cannot measure tech ROI.

Organizations identified as AI Pacesetters—only 9% of those surveyed—are being very intentional about building trust, leaning into change management and redesigning processes around talent capabilities, according to Kyndryl's research.

enContent.subject: The AI landed. Nobody rewrote the job.
enContent.preheader: The passive resistance is not adoption. It's a missing role.
subjectOptions: Your team isn't ready. The job isn't written.
The AI landed. Nobody rewrote the job description.
23% ready: why AI rollouts stall at the role line

sections.heading: The Signal
sections.body:
*This week: the technology is landing faster than the operating model can absorb it, and the workforce is being asked to close the gap.*

- **Strategy:** Volvo Car CEO Håkan Samuelsson pushed back on White House trade adviser Peter Navarro's "pirate" framing of Chinese automakers, calling them the new industry leaders alongside Audi, BMW, and Mercedes. **Strategy is naming which competitors you refuse to see.** [Read →](https://fortune.com/2026/07/20/volvo-car-ceo-pushes-back-on-navarros-pirate-china-comments/)

- **Operating Models:** A Fast Company piece argues that as AI handles routine work, delegation stops being a productivity move and becomes the mechanism that builds judgment in the layer below the owner. **Delegation only compounds when it is designed.** [Read →](https://www.fastcompany.com/91569517/are-you-delegating-wrong-delegating-leadership-management)

- **Technology:** A TD Cowen survey of 80 biopharma leaders finds AI is compressing preclinical drug development costs and timelines by up to 70 percent. **Technology compounds only where the process was already written down.** [Read →](https://www.axios.com/2026/07/20/artificial-intelligence-drug-development-impact)

- **Human Capital:** A Kyndryl study of 1,100 senior leaders across eight countries finds only 23 percent say their workforce is fully ready for AI, a six-point drop in a year. **The workforce is waiting for someone to name the new job.** [Read →](https://hrexecutive.com/kyndryl-chro-3-strategies-to-become-an-ai-pacesetter/)

sections.heading: The Apertura
sections.body:
===OPTION_A:statistic===
Only twenty-three percent of senior leaders say their workforce is fully ready for the AI they have already deployed, [according to a new Kyndryl study of 1,100 leaders across eight countries](https://hrexecutive.com/kyndryl-chro-3-strategies-to-become-an-ai-pacesetter/). Down six points in a single year. Nearly eighty percent say the pace of AI is outrunning their workforce, governance, and operating models. Only nine percent qualify as "Pacesetters." The reading everyone reaches for is a skills problem. Buy more training. Run more workshops. It is not a skills problem. The tools were installed on top of roles nobody rewrote. The team is not slow. The job description was never updated.

sections.heading: The Insight
sections.body:
You bought the AI. The team is using it, sort of. But the work is not moving faster. Decisions still route through you. The people you deployed the tool to sound polite in meetings and unconvinced in private. You call this an adoption problem and schedule more training.

It is not an adoption problem.

The mistake is upstream of the tool. In our experience, leaders deploy AI assuming the existing role definitions will absorb it. They will not. The role that existed before the tool was written for a job the tool has now partially taken over. No one has told the person in the role what their new job actually is. They still see the same title, the same objectives, the same escalation path. The AI shows up like an unpaid intern nobody asked for, doing pieces of their work while the boundaries of ownership stay unchanged.

The passive resistance you are reading as technical friction is something else. It is the rational response of a professional who has not been told what they now own. The tool quietly redistributes their work. No one names the new role. That feels less like an upgrade and more like a demotion in slow motion. Their silence is not confusion. It is self-protection.

This is where the Operating Model OS work begins, before any training deck is opened. The artifact required is a Role Redesign Map. For every role touched by the AI deployment, three questions have to be answered on paper, by name, by the leader. What decisions does this role now own that it did not own before? What work that previously belonged to this role now belongs to the tool? What new judgment must the human exercise on top of what the tool produces?

Those three answers are the new job. Until they exist in writing, you have not deployed AI. You have installed software on top of a workflow the team is now silently negotiating in the dark. The vision speech about AI will not close the gap. Only the redesign will, because it is the redesign that shows the person what the daily work now looks like. Not the strategy behind it.

This week, pick one role where AI is already deployed. Sit down for thirty minutes and answer the three questions. Write it out. Then share it with the person in that role and ask what you got wrong. The conversation that follows is the actual deployment. What you did last quarter was the invoice.

sections.heading: The Field Report
sections.body:
Maria Black marked her 30th year at ADP by becoming its seventh CEO. She [told HR Executive](https://hrexecutive.com/how-adp-ceo-maria-black-leads-through-uncertain-times/) that the top question every CEO is now trying to answer is what a workforce that includes both AI and humans actually looks like. She points to Gartner research showing many HR leaders admit their current technologies do not fully meet business needs, and about half cannot measure the ROI of the tech they have already bought. Her filter is two questions: what is the right thing for clients, and what is the right thing for associates.

For the mid-market operator, the reading of this shift is direct. The problem is not which AI to buy next. The tools already sitting on the balance sheet were installed before anyone rewrote what the associate role now is. Half the market cannot measure the return because the return depends on a role redesign that never happened. In our view, the advantage moves toward the operator willing to do that redesign in writing before signing the next license.

sections.heading: The Tool
sections.body: **Scribe** - A screen-capture tool that turns any workflow you run on your computer into a step-by-step document, automatically. It matters because you cannot redesign a role until you can see what the role actually does today. Find it at [scribehow.com](https://scribehow.com).

sections.heading: The Compass
sections.body: **Watch for this week:** how many times a member of your team asks you a question about a task the AI is supposed to be handling. Every one of those escalations is not a training gap. It is an unwritten line in the role. Count them Monday through Friday. The number is your Role Redesign Map, first draft, delivered by the people who are living inside the gap.

esContent.subject: Llegó la IA. Nadie reescribió el puesto.
esContent.preheader: Lo que usted lee como resistencia es, en realidad, un puesto sin dueño.
esContent.thesis: Las medianas empresas están instalando IA para ganar eficiencia, pero no capturan valor estratégico porque no rediseñaron el modelo operativo que debía sostenerla. El cuello de botella no es la tecnología: es el puesto que nadie volvió a escribir.
esContent.shareableSentence: Hasta que las tres preguntas del rediseño estén por escrito, usted no desplegó IA: instaló software encima de un flujo que el equipo negocia en silencio.

sections.heading: LA SEÑAL
sections.body:
*Esta semana: la tecnología entra más rápido de lo que el modelo operativo alcanza a absorberla, y la carga cae sobre el equipo.*

- **Estrategia:** El CEO de Volvo Car, Håkan Samuelsson, respondió al asesor comercial de la Casa Blanca Peter Navarro, quien tildó de "piratas" a las automotrices chinas, y las colocó como los nuevos líderes de la industria junto a Audi, BMW y Mercedes. **La estrategia empieza por nombrar a los competidores que uno se niega a ver.** [Leer en Fortune ->](https://fortune.com/2026/07/20/volvo-car-ceo-pushes-back-on-navarros-pirate-china-comments/)
- **Modelos Operativos:** Un texto de Fast Company sostiene que, en la medida en que la IA absorbe el trabajo rutinario, delegar deja de ser un truco de productividad y se convierte en el mecanismo que forma criterio en la capa siguiente al dueño. **Delegar solo compone cuando está diseñado.** [Leer en Fast Company ->](https://www.fastcompany.com/91569517/are-you-delegating-wrong-delegating-leadership-management)
- **Tecnología:** Una encuesta de TD Cowen a 80 líderes biofarma concluye que la IA está comprimiendo costos y plazos preclínicos hasta en 70%. **Solo compone donde el proceso ya estaba escrito.** [Leer en Axios Business ->](https://www.axios.com/2026/07/20/artificial-intelligence-drug-development-impact)
- **Capital Humano:** Un estudio de Kyndryl a 1,100 líderes en ocho países encuentra que solo 23% considera a su fuerza de trabajo plenamente lista para la IA, seis puntos menos que hace un año. **El equipo espera que alguien le nombre el nuevo lugar.** [Leer en HR Executive ->](https://hrexecutive.com/kyndryl-chro-3-strategies-to-become-an-ai-pacesetter/)

sections.heading: LA APERTURA
sections.body: Solo 23% de los líderes senior afirma que su equipo está plenamente listo para la IA que ya desplegaron, [según un nuevo estudio de Kyndryl a 1,100 líderes en ocho países](https://hrexecutive.com/kyndryl-chro-3-strategies-to-become-an-ai-pacesetter/). Seis puntos menos en un año. Cerca del 80% admite que la IA avanza más rápido que su fuerza de trabajo, su gobernanza y su modelo operativo. Apenas 9% califica como "Pacesetter". La lectura fácil es que se trata de un problema de habilidades: más capacitación, más talleres. No lo es. Las herramientas se instalaron sobre puestos que nadie reescribió. El equipo no va lento. La descripción del puesto se quedó vieja.

sections.heading: EL INSIGHT
sections.body:
Usted compró la IA. El equipo la usa, más o menos. Y aun así el trabajo no avanza más rápido. Las decisiones siguen llegando a su escritorio. Las personas a quienes entregó la herramienta se ven corteses en las juntas y poco convencidas en privado. Usted lo interpreta como un problema de adopción y agenda otra capacitación.

No es un problema de adopción.

El error está antes de la herramienta. En nuestra experiencia, los líderes despliegan IA suponiendo que las descripciones de puesto existentes van a absorberla. No ocurre así. El puesto que existía antes fue escrito para un trabajo que la herramienta ya se llevó en parte. Nadie le explicó a la persona en ese puesto en qué consiste ahora su trabajo. Sigue viendo el mismo título, los mismos objetivos, la misma ruta de escalamiento. La IA aparece como un becario no solicitado que ejecuta fragmentos de su trabajo mientras las fronteras de propiedad permanecen intactas.

La resistencia pasiva que usted lee como fricción técnica es otra cosa. Es la respuesta racional de un profesional al que nadie le ha dicho qué le pertenece ahora. La herramienta redistribuye su trabajo en silencio. Nadie nombra el nuevo puesto. Eso se siente menos como un ascenso y más como una degradación en cámara lenta. El silencio no es confusión. Es defensa propia.

Aquí empieza el trabajo del Operating Model OS, antes de abrir cualquier presentación de capacitación. El artefacto necesario es un Mapa de Rediseño de Puesto. Para cada puesto afectado por la IA, hay tres preguntas que el líder tiene que responder por escrito, con nombre y apellido: ¿Qué decisiones son ahora responsabilidad de este puesto y antes no lo eran? ¿Qué trabajo que antes realizaba este puesto ahora lo ejecuta la herramienta? ¿Qué juicio nuevo tiene que ejercer la persona sobre lo que la herramienta produce?

Esas tres respuestas son el nuevo puesto. Mientras no estén en papel, usted no desplegó IA: instaló software encima de un flujo que el equipo está negociando en silencio. El discurso sobre la visión de la IA no va a cerrar esa brecha. Solo el rediseño la cierra, porque es el rediseño lo que le muestra a la persona cómo luce el trabajo diario ahora. No la estrategia detrás.

Esta semana, elija un puesto donde la IA ya esté desplegada. Dedique treinta minutos a responder las tres preguntas. Escríbalas. Después compártalas con la persona en ese puesto y pregúntele qué le faltó. La conversación que viene es el despliegue verdadero. Lo del trimestre pasado fue la factura.

sections.heading: EL REPORTE DE CAMPO
sections.body:
## Spotlight

Maria Black cumplió 30 años en ADP y se convirtió en su séptima directora general. [En entrevista con HR Executive](https://hrexecutive.com/how-adp-ceo-maria-black-leads-through-uncertain-times/) señaló que la pregunta que todo director general intenta responder es cómo se ve una fuerza de trabajo que combina humanos e IA. Cita investigación de Gartner: muchos líderes de recursos humanos reconocen que sus tecnologías actuales no cubren del todo las necesidades del negocio, y cerca de la mitad no puede medir el retorno de lo que ya compró. Su filtro son dos preguntas: qué es lo correcto para los clientes y qué es lo correcto para los colaboradores.

Para el operador de mediana empresa, la lectura es directa. El problema no es cuál IA adquirir después. Las herramientas que ya aparecen en el balance se instalaron antes de que nadie redefiniera en qué consiste hoy el puesto del colaborador. La mitad del mercado no puede medir el retorno porque ese retorno depende de un rediseño de puesto que nunca ocurrió. En nuestra opinión, la ventaja se inclina hacia el operador dispuesto a hacer ese rediseño por escrito antes de firmar la siguiente licencia.

sections.heading: LA HERRAMIENTA
sections.body: **Scribe** - Una herramienta de captura de pantalla que convierte cualquier flujo que usted ejecute en la computadora en un documento paso a paso, de forma automática. Resulta útil porque no se puede rediseñar un puesto sin ver primero qué hace ese puesto hoy. Está en [scribehow.com](https://scribehow.com).

sections.heading: LA BRÚJULA
sections.body: **El indicador de la semana:** cuántas veces alguien de su equipo le consulta algo sobre una tarea que la IA supuestamente ya está resolviendo. Cada uno de esos escalamientos no refleja una carencia de capacitación. Es una línea sin escribir en la descripción del puesto. Cuéntelos de lunes a viernes. Ese número es su Mapa de Rediseño de Puesto, primer borrador, entregado por quienes viven dentro de la brecha.

sections.heading: LA PUERTA
sections.body:
Si algo de esta publicación resonó contigo - platiquemos en [Instagram](https://ig.me/m/wbardawil)
Si le es útil a alguien en tu red - reenvialo por [WhatsApp](https://wa.me/?text=Checa%20este%20newsletter%3A%20https%3A%2F%2Fnewsletter.wadibardawil.com)
Cuando estés listo para trabajar juntos directamente - descubre mis 4 formas de trabajo en [wadibardawil.com](https://wadibardawil.com)

assets.prompt: A hand-drawn editorial illustration showing a pristine architect's blueprint on a drafting table, viewed from above. The blueprint's clean lines fade into incomplete sketches at the edges. A single mechanical pencil rests diagonally across the paper, suspended mid-stroke, as if the designer walked away before defining the critical elements. In the negative space beyond the blueprint, faint pencil marks suggest erased attempts—ghosted job titles, crossed-out org charts, abandoned role definitions. One small section of the blueprint is highlighted in orange: an empty box labeled "role" with no connecting lines. Sophisticated pencil sketch style. Editorial newspaper quality. White background. Minimal composition. Strategic absence as the visual statement. Include small WB logo in corner.

altText.en: Blueprint architectural drawing overlaid with translucent digital interface screens showing disconnected workflow nodes
altText.es: Plano arquitectónico con pantallas digitales translúcidas superpuestas mostrando nodos de flujo de trabajo desconectados

caption.en: You deployed the technology, but the operating model still runs on last decade's logic.
caption.es: Implementaste la tecnología, pero el modelo operativo sigue corriendo con la lógica de la década pasada.
